questions.question: Which chart type is best for showing how monthly revenue changes over a year?
options: Pie chart
Stacked bar
Line chart
Doughnut chart
questions.explanation: Line charts are designed for ordered time series. Pie/doughnut are for share-of-total at a single point in time and don't show change well.

questions.question: You want to show revenue (in dollars) and units sold (in counts) on the same chart. Best choice?
options: Two separate line charts side by side
A combo chart with units on a secondary axis
A stacked bar chart
A 3D pie chart
questions.explanation: Combo charts let two series share the same x-axis but use different y-axis scales — essential when the numbers are on very different magnitudes.

questions.question: What does a color scale conditional format do?
options: Changes the font colour of headers
Colours cells along a gradient based on their value (low → high)
Hides cells below a threshold
Locks the cell colour to its category
questions.explanation: Color scales (Home → Conditional Formatting → Color Scales) turn a numeric grid into a heat map — a quick way to spot hot and cold spots.

questions.question: What is a sparkline?
options: A type of chart that lives inside a single cell
A line chart with extra data labels
A 3D version of a bar chart
A formula that returns chart data
questions.explanation: Sparklines are tiny in-cell charts — perfect for showing a trend per row in a long table without taking up dashboard space.

questions.question: When is a horizontal bar chart better than a vertical column chart?
options: Always — bars are more modern
When you have many categories with long labels
Only for percentages
Never — columns are always preferred
questions.explanation: Long category labels (like full customer names) read more easily next to horizontal bars. Vertical columns crowd labels at angles.
